perturbation.original_text: 8. This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced pursuant to the laws and judicial precedents of the State of California, without reference to principles of conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text:
11. Neither Party makes any representations or warranties as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information and neither Party shall have any liability to the other resulting from any use of the Confidential Information which is consistent with this Agreement.

8. This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced pursuant to the laws and judicial precedents of the State of Delaware, without reference to principles of conflicts of law.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the choice of law provision to a different section and altering the specified state to Delaware obfuscates the governing law of the contract. Placing it after a liability disclaimer makes it less prominent.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1646 states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. By changing the designated state and moving the provision, the contract attempts to circumvent California law, particularly if the contract was formed and is expected to be performed in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies California law, while the changed text specifies Delaware law. According to the perturbation explanation, the choice of law provision has been moved and the state has been altered. The Law Explanation states that California Civil Code Section 1646 dictates contract interpretation based on the place of performance or formation. The Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that "A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made." Changing the state to Delaware potentially contradicts California law if the contract was formed and is expected to be performed in California, because it could be seen as circumventing California law. This falls under Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction, because the legally required choice of law provision has been placed in a different area and also altered.

perturbation.original_text: 5. All written data delivered by Owner to the Recipient pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of Owner, and all such written data, and all copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to Owner upon written request, or destroyed at Owner's option.
perturbation.changed_text:
11. Neither Party makes any representations or warranties as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information and neither Party shall have any liability to the other resulting from any use of the Confidential Information which is consistent with this Agreement.

5. All written data delivered by Owner to the Recipient pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of Owner, and all such written data, and all copies thereof, shall be returned to Owner at Owner's option.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the return of data clause after the liability disclaimer obscures the owner's right to immediate return of their proprietary information by moving it to a less visible location. The removal of 'destroyed at Owner's option' removes owner's recourse after it is returned. In addition, the removal of 'promptly' weakens the enforcement of the request.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By weakening the clause regarding the return of confidential data, especially concerning the timeline and possible destruction of data, the contract makes it harder for the Owner to enforce their rights under CUTSA if the Recipient were to misappropriate the information. The data owner loses the right to the immediate return of documents.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The altered contract weakens the data owner's ability to protect their trade secrets, potentially violating CUTSA. The original contract mandated prompt return or destruction of data, whereas the modified version weakens the 'prompt' return requirement and removes the option for destruction of data entirely. The Law Explanation indicates this change makes it harder for the Owner to enforce their rights under CUTSA if the Recipient were to misappropriate the information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), supporting that the information is a trade secret, and the weakening of the data return clause contradicts CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: 1. One party hereto ("Owner") may disclose to the other party hereto ("Recipient") certain Confidential Information pursuant to this Agreement which Owner deems proprietary and confidential. The Parties agree that Recipient shall not use and shall prevent the disclosure of any information it receives from Owner that is marked PROPRIETARY AND CONFIDENTIAL, or similarly marked, or any other information (whether delivered in writing or verbally) which by its nature would be reasonably considered as confidential, to any other person, firm or corporation or delivered in connection with the evaluation of the Transaction, except as provided herein, and shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure of such information as Recipient employs with respect to its own Confidential Information of like importance.
perturbation.changed_text:
13. Nothing in this Agreement shall impose any obligation upon the Parties hereto to consummate a Transaction or to enter into any discussion or negotiations with respect thereto.

1. One party hereto ("Owner") may disclose to the other party hereto ("Recipient") certain Confidential Information pursuant to this Agreement which Owner deems proprietary and confidential. The Parties agree that Recipient shall not use and shall not disclose any information it receives from Owner that is marked PROPRIETARY AND CONFIDENTIAL, or similarly marked, to any other person, firm or corporation or delivered in connection with the evaluation of the Transaction, except as provided herein, and shall use a reasonable degree of care to avoid disclosure of such information.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the confidentiality obligation to after the 'no obligation to transact' clause makes the duty to protect confidential information appear less critical. Furthermore, the removal of 'prevent the disclosure of any information...which by its nature would be reasonably considered as confidential' significantly narrows the scope of confidentiality, only protecting explicitly marked information.  Also, by removing 'same degree of care to avoid disclosure of such information as Recipient employs with respect to its own Confidential Information of like importance', we weaken the legal protection of the data.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1644 states that words in a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense. By limiting the confidential information only to explicitly marked information and moving this to the later part of the agreement, the contract contradicts the principle that confidentiality extends to information reasonably understood to be confidential. It now is unclear that a trade secret must be protected.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1644 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1644. The words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning; unless used by the parties in a technical sense, or unless a special meaning is given to them by usage, in which case the latter must be followed. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modification contradicts California Civil Code Section 1644. The original text protects both explicitly marked confidential information and information that 'by its nature would be reasonably considered as confidential.' The changed text narrows the scope of confidentiality to only 'information it receives from Owner that is marked PROPRIETARY AND CONFIDENTIAL, or similarly marked'. This contradicts the law's instruction that words in a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense because the changed text removes the protection of information reasonably understood to be confidential, weakening the legal standard. The law snippet states 'The words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning.' By explicitly limiting confidentiality to only marked information, the modified contract disregards the "ordinary and popular sense" understanding of what constitutes confidential information, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Notwithstanding the provisions of Paragraph 1 above, the Parties may disclose the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement to their employees and/or agents, but only for the purpose of supplying the Party with sufficient information to enable the Party to evaluate the potential value of establishing a business and/or contractual relationship with each other. The Recipient will inform each of its employees, agents and affiliates (collectively, "Representatives") who will receive Confidential Information of the obligations under this Agreement and agrees to take all commercially reasonable measures to restrain its Representatives from taking any action that would constitute a breach of the terms of this Agreement. In any event, the Recipient shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text:
6. This Agreement shall be binding on, and shall inure to the benefit of, the Parties hereto, their heirs, successors, and assigns.

2. Notwithstanding the provisions of Paragraph 1 above, the Parties may disclose the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement to their employees and/or agents, but only for the purpose of supplying the Party with sufficient information to enable the Party to evaluate the potential value of establishing a business and/or contractual relationship with each other. The Recipient agrees to take all commercially reasonable measures to restrain its Representatives from taking any action that would constitute a breach of the terms of this Agreement. In any event, the Recipient shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'permitted disclosure' clause after the 'binding agreement' clause obscures the scope of permissible disclosure and limits the obligations regarding the information, making it seem like an afterthought. Also, the clause that says: 'The Recipient will inform each of its employees, agents and affiliates (collectively, "Representatives") who will receive Confidential Information of the obligations under this Agreement' is removed. This is contradictory.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 2351 states that an agent has authority to do everything necessary or proper and usual, in the ordinary course of business, for effecting the purpose of his agency. By removing the requirement to inform employees of the obligations, the contract weakens the principal's control over the agent and makes it more difficult for the principal to ensure the confidentiality is maintained. This can be seen as restricting an agent's implied authority, which needs to be explicitly stated in the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 2351 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. AGENCY [2295 - 2400] ( Title 9 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Agency in General [2295 - 2357] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 5. Delegation of Agency [2349 - 2351] ( Article 5 enacted 1872. ) 2351. A sub-agent, lawfully appointed, represents the principal in like manner with the original agent; and the original agent is not responsible to third persons for the acts of the sub-agent. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit obligation for the recipient to inform its representatives (employees, agents, affiliates) of their confidentiality obligations under the agreement. This removal weakens the principal's ability to control the agent and ensure confidentiality. The Law Explanation points to California Civil Code Section 2351, stating that an agent has the authority to do everything necessary for the agency's purpose. By not informing the representatives of the confidentiality obligations, the principal's control is weakened, potentially restricting the agent's implied authority. The perturbation is categorized as 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. In this case, the removal of the clause has the same effect. The scraped snippet defines a sub-agent lawfully appointed represents the principal in like manner with the original agent. By removing the requirement to inform the agents of their responsibility, the agent's ability to lawfully represent the principal is damaged.
